project_acceptance.observable: vault.schema.SCHEMA_VERSION == 3 and MIGRATIONS registers migration 002 (target version 2, adds pinned) and migration 003 (target version 3, adds word_count).
The committed data/notes.json is at "schema_version": 3 and every note carries pinned == false and the correct word_count (n-1: 7, n-2: 10, n-3: 2).
tools.build_index.build_index emits id, title, pinned and word_count per entry, still sorted by note id, still under a source_schema_version key.
The committed data/index.json is the builder's output for the migrated vault: "source_schema_version": 3, and each entry carrying that note's real pinned (false) and word_count (7 / 10 / 2).
`python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes and covers the migrations.

tasks.objective: In vault/schema.py add migration 002: a function taking the vault dict that gives every note a `pinned` field set to the boolean False. Register it in MIGRATIONS as target version 2. The migration must not touch schema_version itself — the migrator sets that.

context_refs.claim: The module docstring specifies how MIGRATIONS entries are written: (target_version, function) in ascending order, and that a migration edits the vault dict in place and must never set schema_version itself.
SCHEMA_VERSION is 1 today and MIGRATIONS is empty.

may_change: vault/schema.py (add the migration function, register it in MIGRATIONS)

acceptance.observable: MIGRATIONS contains an entry with target version 2 whose function sets note["pinned"] = False on every note in the vault dict.
The value is the boolean False, not the string "false" and not 0.

tasks.handoff: Migration 002 exists and is registered at target version 2.

tasks.objective: In vault/schema.py add migration 003: a function taking the vault dict that gives every note a `word_count` field, the number of whitespace-separated words in that note's body (len(body.split())). Register it in MIGRATIONS as target version 3, after migration 002.

context_refs.claim: MIGRATIONS is an ascending list of (target_version, function) pairs, so migration 003 is registered after migration 002.
Each note has a body string; the three committed notes have bodies of 7, 10 and 2 words (n-1, n-2, n-3).

may_change: vault/schema.py (add the migration function, register it in MIGRATIONS)

acceptance.observable: MIGRATIONS contains an entry with target version 3, listed after the target-2 entry, whose function sets note["word_count"] = len(note["body"].split()) on every note.
Running both migrations over the committed notes yields word_count 7 for n-1, 10 for n-2 and 2 for n-3.

tasks.handoff: Both migrations exist and are registered in ascending order (2 then 3).

tasks.objective: Set vault.schema.SCHEMA_VERSION to 3: the version of the vault the code now expects.

context_refs.claim: The existing migration tests are written against SCHEMA_VERSION rather than a hard-coded number, so they keep working when it moves.

must_preserve: the registered migrations and their target versions

tasks.handoff: The code expects vault schema version 3.

tasks.objective: Bring the checked-in vault up to date: run `python3 -m vault.migrate data/notes.json` from the project root so the committed data/notes.json is the migrator's output. Do not hand-edit the vault file.

context_refs.claim: `python3 -m vault.migrate data/notes.json` applies every migration registered above the file's current schema_version and writes the vault back; against an already-migrated file it does nothing.
apply_migrations only runs the migrations that are registered in MIGRATIONS at the moment it is called; migrations registered later are not applied retroactively.
The committed vault is still at schema_version 1.

must_preserve: data/notes.json must be the migrator's output — no hand edits
the note ids, titles, bodies and created dates already in the vault

acceptance.observable: data/notes.json was rewritten by the migrator, not by hand.
Running `python3 -m vault.migrate data/notes.json` again reports the vault is already up to date and leaves the file unchanged.
The vault still holds the same three notes with their original ids, titles, bodies and created dates.

tasks.handoff: The committed vault is the migrator's output for the registered migrations.

tasks.objective: Teach the index builder about the new note fields: tools.build_index.build_index must put id, title, pinned and word_count in each entry, reading them from the note and falling back to False / 0 for a note that does not carry the field. Keep the rest of the shape as it is: a source_schema_version key taken from the vault, and an entries list sorted by note id.

context_refs.claim: build_index currently emits only id and title per entry, sorts entries by note id, and copies the vault's schema_version into source_schema_version.
The index is generated from data/notes.json and is only ever as fresh as the vault it was built from.

must_preserve: the entries stay sorted by note id
source_schema_version keeps coming from the vault's schema_version

acceptance.observable: A note lacking pinned / word_count still indexes, as False / 0.
The index document still has source_schema_version and an entries list ordered by note id.

tasks.handoff: The builder knows how to index pinned and word_count.

tasks.objective: Rebuild the committed index: run `python3 -m tools.build_index` from the project root so data/index.json is the builder's output for the vault. Do not hand-edit data/index.json.

context_refs.claim: `python3 -m tools.build_index` reads data/notes.json and rewrites data/index.json; the index is only as fresh as the vault it was built from.
data/index.json is a generated file, only as fresh as the vault it was built from.

must_preserve: tools/build_index.py: this task changes no code
data/index.json must be the builder's output — no hand edits

acceptance.observable: data/index.json was rewritten by the builder, not by hand.
Re-running `python3 -m tools.build_index` leaves data/index.json unchanged, i.e. the committed index already matches the committed vault.
data/index.json holds one entry per note, ordered by note id.

tasks.handoff: The committed index is the builder's output for the committed vault.

tasks.objective: Cover the migrations with tests under tests/: starting from a vault at schema_version 1, applying the migrations gives every note pinned == False and word_count == len(body.split()), and lands the vault at schema_version 3. `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` must pass.

context_refs.claim: sample_vault(schema_version) builds an in-memory vault, so migration behaviour can be tested without touching data/notes.json.
apply_migrations returns the list of target versions it applied and leaves the vault at the last one.

must_preserve: the existing idempotence and current-version tests keep passing
vault/schema.py and vault/migrate.py: this task adds no production code

acceptance.observable: `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes with no failures.
A test asserts that migrating a schema_version 1 vault sets pinned == False on every note.
A test asserts word_count == len(body.split()) after migrating, e.g. 3 for a body of "one two three".

assumptions.statement: Word counts are plain whitespace splits (len(body.split())): no punctuation stripping, no locale rules.
Notes are pinned by an operator later; the migration only introduces the field, so every existing note starts unpinned.
